_comment: Chapter 2 (Syllables & Accents), reconstructed from 2_ACCENT.TBK strings + CHAPT_2 audio inventory (2026-07-20). Instruction texts and teaching content extracted verbatim where string extraction reached them; rich-text-only material is flagged _verify. Sequence is DERIVED FROM PEDAGOGY and NOT yet DOSBox-verified. New mode/type proposals (topicPages, divide, placeAccent, greekRows/expander blocks) are itemized in 5B-SPEC.md.
title: Syllables & Accents
objectives: identify syllables for pronunciation and accents
recognize the basic rules of Greek accents
identify rough/smooth breathings, apostrophes and diaeresis markings
remember English grammar (parts of speech, noun declension and verb parsing)

content.text: In order to correctly pronounce Greek words we need to be able to identify the syllables of the words.

Greek divides words into syllables in almost the same way as English. So if you don't recognize a new word, just try to pronounce it as you would in English. Generally, start at the left and divide after the vowel.
(Mounce, pp. 15ff; Summers, pp. 8, Hewett, p. 4)

items.text: There is one vowel or diphthong per syllable. A lone consonant often goes with the following vowel.
Two consonants in a row are divided (except when the second consonant is n or a consonant pair that is used to begin words).
Two vowels (two diphthongs or a vowel and a diphthong) in a row are split unless the two vowels form a diphthong.

content.label: Examples
content._verify: The original has example popups (OneVowel / TwoConsonants / TwoVowels buttons) whose word content is stored in rich-text records and was not string-extractable. Need DOSBox screenshots of each of the three example popups.

content.text: Traditionally the last three syllables have had specific names. The last syllable is called the "ultima," the second from the last the "penult" and the third from the last the "antepenult."

Accents originally indicated the pitch of one's voice but today we use them to denote stress. While accents were not part of the original text, they are helpful for consistent pronunciation and may, at points, differentiate between two similar words.
We will simply learn to recognize them and use them to standardize pronunciation.
(Mounce, pp. 14f.; Summers, p. 9; Machen, pp. 14f.)

items.text: Acute ( ́ ): angles upward originally indicating a rising pitch.
Grave ( ̀ ): angles downward originally indicating a falling pitch.
Circumflex ( ͂ ): angles upward then downward originally indicating a rising then falling pitch.

content.text: Brief Background of Accents
The first accents are seen in the fourth century B.C. and were standarized around 200 B.C. They are not found in the earliest New Testament manuscripts until after 500 A.D.
(Hewett, p. 4)

items.text: Acute may occur on any of the last three syllables.
Circumflex may occur only on the last two syllables (but only if the vowel is long.)
Grave may occur only on the last syllable.

content.text: (Machen, p. 14; Mounce, p. 20; Summers, p. 9)

content.label: Acute on any of the last three syllables
Circumflex on either of the last two long syllables

content.text: Diphthongs are considered long except for final syllable oi or ai.

items.text: Nouns are retentive. They attempt to keep their accents on the same syllable.
Verbs are recessive. Their accent recedes towards the first syllable as far as is possible.
If the ultima is long, then the antepenult cannot be accented.
If the ultima is long and the penult is accented, then the accent must be an acute.
If the ultima is short and the penult is both long and accented, the accent must be a circumflex.
Acute on the last syllable is changed to a grave when followed by another word.

content.text: (Machen, pp. 14f.; Mounce, p. 20)

content.label: Rule 3: Long Ultima, no antepenult accent

content._verify: The Rule 4 chart region also references audio id ex2_15 -- an exercise-pool word likely appears as a further example row here. DOSBox screenshot of the Rule 4 chart needed.

content.label: Rule 5: Short Ultima, long penult = circumflex

content._verify: b_ac1..b_ac3 pairing inferred from chart adjacency; listen-check

content.label: Rule 6: Acute Ultima changed to grave

content.text: There are several short Greek words which do not have an accent. These words are pronounced as if they were part of the word which accompanies them.

items.text: Proclitic: comes before the word which carries the accent.
Enclitic: comes after the word which carries the accent.

learn.title: Learn Other Marks

content.text: There are two breathing marks which are placed on vowels and diphthongs when they begin words.

items.text: Smooth breathing ( ̓ ): It does not affect pronunciation.
Rough breathing ( ̔ ): Adds an "h" before the sound of the initial vowel.

content.text: NOTE: an initial rho ( ῥ ) also takes a rough breathing.
(Mounce, p. 10; Machen, p. 11; Summers, pp. 8f.)

content._verify: huper (b_huper) and rema (b_rema) sit adjacent to the breathing examples in the TBK; likely further example rows on the popups. DOSBox screenshots of the SmoothBreathing and RoughBreathing popups needed.

content.text: There are four punctuation marks in Greek. The comma and period are the same as in English.
(Mounce, pp. 13f.; Summers, p. 8; Machen, p. 12)

In English, letters that drop out (elide) are marked with an apostrophe (e.g. it's). Greek also uses an apostrophe to mark the missing letter(s). The final letter of a preposition, if it is a vowel, is dropped when it precedes a word that begins with a vowel.
(Note the "a" lost is replaced by an apostrophe; Jn 1:3,7 cf. Jn 1:39)

content._verify: παρ᾽ αὐτῷ (b_paraut) appears in this page's region -- likely an additional apostrophe example. Confirm placement via DOSBox.

content.text: Sometimes a final vowel word followed by an initial vowel word will contract together. This is called "Crasis." A coronis ( ̓ ) is used to retain the breathing of the second word.
(Summers, p. 8)
A diaeresis is placed over the second of two vowels in a row to show that the vowels are to be pronounced separately and not combined as a diphthong. This often occurs in personal or place names.

learn.title: Learn Grammar Review

items.text: Noun: names a person, place, thing or idea (e.g. book).
Adjective: a word used to qualify the meaning of the noun (e.g. good book).
Definite Article: a word that specifies a particular noun (e.g. the book). The Indefinite Article is "a".
Pronoun: a word used instead of a noun (e.g. the book, it).
Preposition: a relational word that connects an object (often a noun) to its antecedent (e.g. in the book).
Verb: often an action or state of being word which makes a statement, asks a question, or gives a command (e.g. run).
Adverb: qualifies the meaning of the verb (e.g. run quickly).
Conjunction: a word that joins words, clauses or sentences (e.g. and, but).

content.text: (See any English Grammar handbook for help in this area. (cf. Wenham, pp. 1ff).

items.def: I, you, he, she, we, it
this, that
mine, yours, ours, theirs
who? which? what?
one another
who, which, that

content.text: The sentence is divided into two parts:

items.text: subject--about which something is said, and
predicate--that which is said about the subject.

content.text: A phrase is a group of words used as a single part of speech.
A clause is a group of words which includes a subject and predicate.

items.def: Terry went to the store. / The big red truck moved slowly.
The big red truck moved slowly.
Terry and Dawn went to the store.
Please close the door. ("you" is understood)
Joy walked home.
Joy walked home.
Joy walked home and raked leaves.
It is I. (rather than "It is me.")

content.text: A predicate nominative completes the idea of the subject. It will most often occur with an "is" verb.

content._verify: Simple/Complete Predicate rows show identical example text in the extraction ('Joy walked home.') -- the original likely underlines different spans; confirm exact rendering via screenshot.

content.text: Perhaps the most common is the prepositional phrase:

items.def: Students are often late in the morning. (Acts like an adjective)
He held the book over his head. (Acts like an adverb)

content.text: Infinitive phrases often act as nouns:

items.def: With work you can expect to master Greek. (as a noun)
He played to win. (as an adverb)
He had plenty of water to drink. (as an adjective modifying water)
The great big strong man (an adjective phrase)
The man who owns the store (an adjectival/relative clause)

content.text: A main clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone. A subordinate clause is dependent on the main clause and cannot stand alone. Note the following subordinate clauses.

items.def: When the store opened the people pushed through the front door.
He knew that power had gone out of him.
Joy walks everyday. (Present tense)
Joy walked yesterday. (Past tense)
Joy will walk tomorrow. (Future Tense)
I was studying for a week for the test. (Process)
I studied for the test.
I have studied for the test. (Completed action with continuing effects)

content.text: (Mounce, pp. 118ff)

items.def: generally describes the time of action (Present, Future, Past). However, some Greek tenses are used to denote aspect, or type of action, rather than time.
denotes the type of action: Continuous action (the event as a process), Undefined (the event happened) or Perfect (the event happened with effects continuing into the present).
Active: subject does the action, Middle: subject does action on or for itself, Passive: subject receives the action.
how something is said. Indicative: statement of fact; Subjunctive: desire, probable; Imperative: command; Optative: wish, remote possibility.

rows.label: Voice of a Verb
rows.gloss: Zachary shot the ball. (Active) / The ball was shot by Zachary. (Passive) / Zachary got a ball for himself. (Middle)

rows.label: Mood of a Verb
rows.gloss: Indicative: He learned Greek well. / Subjunctive: In order that he might learn Greek well... If he studies he may learn Greek well. / Imperative: Learn Greek well. / Optative: O that you might learn Greek well.

items.def: I, we = first person; you = second person; he, she, it, they = third person
Singular: I, you, he, she, it; Plural: we, they, you (all)

content.text: (Story & Story, pp. 9-13)
Nouns in Greek have gender, number and case.

items.def: The Greek masculine, feminine, and neuter genders are often indicated by the endings attached to the noun. Abstract nouns and objects that are neither male nor female in English are often marked as either masculine or feminine in Greek.
as an "s" often marks an English word as being plural, likewise, Greek endings mark whether a noun is singular or plural (e.g. book, books).

content.text: Case: In English we have three cases which are seen in how we use our pronouns.

items.text: Subjective or Nominative Case: He=subject (e.g. He did it.)
Objective or Accusative Case: Him = object (e.g. The car hit him.)
Possessive or Genitive Case: His=possessive (e.g. It was his car.)

content.text: Greek adds two more:

items.def: the case marking the indirect object (e.g. I told the story to the apostles.)
the case of direct address. (e.g. O Lord, save me.)

content.text: Endings will be added to the Greek nouns to indicate gender, number and case.

learn.title: Learn Bibliography

content.text: Learn Bibliography

items.text: Hewett, James A. New Testament Greek: A Beginning and Intermediate Grammar (Peabody: Hendrickson, 1986), p. 4.
Machen, J. Gresham. New Testament Greek for Beginners (Toronto: The Macmillan Company, 1923), pp. 11-18.
Mounce, William D. Basics of Biblical Greek: Grammar (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1993), pp. 13-17.
Summers, Ray and Thomas Sawyer. Essentials of New Testament Greek (Nashville: Broadman & Holman, 1995), pp. 8-9.
Wenham, J. W. The Elements of New Testament Greek (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1965), pp. 21-23.

learn._typo_note: TBK prints 'Hewitt, James A.' in the bibliography but cites '(Hewett, p. 4)' in body text; the scholar is James A. Hewett, so the bibliography spelling is corrected per typo policy A1 (scholar names only). Flagged in VERIFY.

items.sentence: Learning it takes time and effort.
In a year you will get it.
Happily study and it will come.
It will also sharpen your English.
But you must do the work.
Relax and enjoy it over a coke.
The good book was written in Greek.

quickReview.title: Review Syllable Rules

content.text: Three Syllable Rules

items.text: There is one vowel or diphthong per syllable. A lone consonant often goes with the following vowel.
Two consonants in a row are divided (except when the second consonant is n or a consonant pair that is used to begin words).
Two vowels (two diphthongs or a vowel and a diphthong) in a row are split unless the two vowels form a diphthong.

quickReview._verify: Chart assumed to reprint the learn-page rules verbatim (the TBK stores one copy reachable from both); confirm the QR chart adds nothing (e.g. example words).

content.text: 6 Accent Rules

items.text: Nouns are retentive. They attempt to keep their accents on the same syllable.
Verbs are recessive. Their accent recedes towards the first syllable as far as is possible.
If the ultima is long, then the antepenult cannot be accented.
If the ultima is long and the penult is accented, then the accent must be an acute.
If the ultima is short and the penult is both long and accented, the accent must be a circumflex.
Acute on the last syllable is changed to a grave when followed by another word.

content.text: Potential Placement: Acute--last 3 syllables
